title: Constraint-Market Compatibility: NAICS 622110 — General Medical and Surgical Hospitals

sections.title: Market Context
sections.content:
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **NAICS code** | 622110 |
| **Market** | General Medical and Surgical Hospitals |
| **Market priority** | HIGH — architecture distance 2 (Rank 3 in 02b) |
| **Product entry point** | L5 — Emergency Exit Security Device (under L6h1 Facility & Building Safety) |
| **Key use cases** | Elopement prevention (psychiatric/dementia/pediatric), restricted-zone perimeter control, compliance documentation |
| **Fire strategy** | Defend-in-place (NFPA 101 §18) — horizontal relocation to adjacent smoke compartment, NOT full evacuation |

---

sections.title: Screening Result
sections.content:
**Market status: SURVIVING — no knockouts.**

| Verdict | Count | Constraints |
|---|---|---|
| knockout | 0 | — |
| mitigable | 7 | C3, C4, C6, C7, C8, C9, C10 |
| none | 3 | C1, C2, C5 |

---

sections.title: Step 08 — Per-Constraint Assessment
sections.content:
| # | Constraint | Type | is_absolute | Verdict | Cost | Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | Emergency Escape Non-Impedance | regulatory | false | **none** | — | — |
| C2 | Mechanical Alarm Independence | regulatory | true | **none** | — | — |
| C3 | Wireless Range Limitation | physical | false | **mitigable** | medium | 2–4 weeks/wing |
| C4 | IoT Battery Life Floor | operational | false | **mitigable** | low | 1–2 months |
| C5 | Retrofit-Only Surface Mount | physical | false | **none** | — | — |
| C6 | EasyWave Protocol Lock-In | operational | false | **mitigable** | medium | 3–6 months |
| C7 | IoT Price Premium | economic | false | **mitigable** | medium | 1–3 months |
| C8 | GDPR / HIPAA Monitoring Data | regulatory | false | **mitigable** | low | 2–4 months |
| C9 | IP Protection Boundary | environmental | false | **mitigable** | low | 2–3 months |
| C10 | EN 13637 / EltVTR Electrical Compliance | regulatory | false | **mitigable** | medium | 3–6 months |

---

sections.title: Constraint Detail
sections.content:
### C1 — Emergency Escape Non-Impedance → **none**

Product is certified to EN 179 (lever, ≤70 N) and EN 1125 (panic bar, single motion). Hospital fire codes (NFPA 101 §18/19, EN 179/1125) require the identical standard. No gap — product is already compliant by design and certification.

---

### C2 — Mechanical Alarm Independence → **none**

The only absolute constraint. Product satisfies it by architecture: mechanical trigger is hardwired, alarm operates at 0 V without battery. Hospital environments present no condition that degrades this guarantee. Battery depletion disables only the IoT module — mechanical alarm remains active.

---

### C3 — Wireless Range Limitation → **mitigable**

Large hospital buildings (200–500 beds) span multiple floors and wings; typical distance from door to nearest gateway frequently exceeds 30 m. Reinforced concrete, HVAC metallic ductwork, and lead-lined radiology rooms further attenuate 868 MHz signals.

| Mitigation action | Detail |
|---|---|
| Deploy wireless repeater network | One repeater per 30 m span in long corridors; budget 1 repeater per wing section |
| RF site survey pre-installation | Identify attenuation zones (radiology, sub-basements, plant rooms) before gateway placement |
| Additional gateway units per zone | Install one gateway per floor/wing cluster for hospital-scale deployments |

**Cost:** medium ($10K–$100K for full hospital coverage) | **Time:** 2–4 weeks per wing

---

### C4 — IoT Battery Life Floor → **mitigable**

High door-use frequency in hospital contexts (ED, ICU corridors, psychiatric ward perimeter, staff access) can deplete a 9V/500 mAh battery well below the 2-year target. Infrequent maintenance access in locked plant rooms and restricted clinical areas creates risk of undetected battery failure.

| Mitigation action | Detail |
|---|---|
| Battery low-alert monitoring | Activate IoT dashboard battery-status alerts; automated escalation to facility management |
| Quarterly replacement schedule for high-frequency doors | ED entrance, ICU stairwells, psychiatric ward exits classified as Tier 1 (quarterly); standard exits Tier 2 (annual) |
| Low-power TX configuration | Reduce event-reporting frequency during overnight low-activity periods via firmware config |

**Cost:** low (<$10K — monitoring software + labor scheduling) | **Time:** 1–2 months

---

### C5 — Retrofit-Only Surface Mount → **none**

Hospital emergency exit doors are predominantly standard fire-rated steel doors in stairwells, clinical corridors, and ward exits where surface mounting is accepted practice. Visible device provides active deterrent in wards with elopement risk (psychiatric, dementia, pediatric). Premium aesthetics are not a hospital procurement criterion for fire-rated exit hardware.

---

### C6 — EasyWave Protocol Lock-In → **mitigable**

Hospital BMS platforms (Siemens Desigo, Honeywell EBI, Schneider EcoStruxure) operate on BACnet/IP. Nurse-call systems (Rauland, Hill-Rom/Baxter) use proprietary protocols. EasyWave has zero native interoperability with any of these. Hospital procurement committees evaluate vendor lock-in risk as a medium-term cost concern.

| Mitigation action | Detail |
|---|---|
| GfS wireless BUS Interface as BACnet bridge | Deploy BUS Interface module; map door-state events to BACnet object types for BMS integration |
| CMMS/CAFM integration module | Develop API connector between GfS monitoring software and hospital CMMS (e.g., Archibus, IBM Maximo) for work-order generation on battery alerts |
| Integration documentation for JCI / JCAHO audits | Provide BMS integration schema as part of compliance documentation package |

**Cost:** medium ($10K–$100K integration development + hardware) | **Time:** 3–6 months

---

### C7 — IoT Price Premium → **mitigable**

A 200-bed hospital with 100–150 emergency exit doors represents ~€73K–€110K hardware spend (at 730 EUR/door). This is within healthcare capital procurement thresholds but requires a structured ROI case. Manual inspection labor costs (quarterly rounds across all exits), compliance audit preparation, and liability insurance are quantifiable offsets.

| Mitigation action | Detail |
|---|---|
| Formal ROI model | Quantify: eliminated manual patrol labor (hrs/year × wage), compliance audit time savings, insurance premium reduction |
| Volume pricing negotiation | Whole-hospital deployment contract; target <2× basic mechanical cost at 50+ door volumes |
| Phased rollout | Highest-risk zones first (psychiatric ward, pharmacy, ED) — lower initial capital, prove value before full deployment |

**Cost:** medium (procurement cycle investment) | **Time:** 1–3 months

---

### C8 — GDPR / HIPAA Monitoring Data Compliance → **mitigable**

Door-open timestamps in psychiatric wards, oncology units, and pediatric zones may constitute personal data under GDPR (EU) if attributable to identifiable individuals (patients, staff). EU hospitals require DPIA for systematic monitoring. US hospitals: HIPAA Business Associate Agreement required if monitoring SaaS could access PHI-adjacent records.

| Mitigation action | Detail |
|---|---|
| Data minimization | Store event type + timestamp + door ID only; no patient/staff identifiers in door-event logs |
| Data Processing Agreement (DPA) | Execute DPA with cloud monitoring SaaS provider before go-live (EU requirement) |
| DPIA for EU sites / BAA for US sites | Complete DPIA for systematic employee/patient monitoring; execute HIPAA BAA with US hospital IT |

**Cost:** low (<$10K legal/compliance effort) | **Time:** 2–4 months

---

### C9 — IP Protection Boundary → **mitigable**

Standard hospital areas (corridors, stairwells, clinical wards) are dry — IP45 outdoor variant is adequate. However: standard indoor variant has no published IP class, and hospital cleaning protocols include wet mopping. Specialty wet areas (sluice rooms, decontamination zones, dirty utility rooms, hospital kitchen exits) require IP65+.

| Mitigation action | Detail |
|---|---|
| Specify IP45 as hospital minimum | Use Art. 990000-IP for all hospital installations regardless of location (over-spec the dry areas, meets wet-corridor cleaning protocols) |
| IP65 enclosure for wet zones | Identify or procure IP65/IP67-rated sealed enclosure variant for sluice rooms, decontamination areas |
| Confirm EMC immunity | Verify IoT module meets medical device EMC immunity (IEC 61326-1) to co-exist with medical imaging RF environments |

**Cost:** low (<$10K incremental — IP45 as standard spec) | **Time:** 2–3 months (specification review)

---

### C10 — EN 13637 / EltVTR Electrical Compliance → **mitigable**

Hospitals combine access control (electromagnetic door holders, electronic locks — ASSA ABLOY Aperio, dormakaba) with emergency exit functionality in psychiatric wards, pharmacies, ICU, and restricted clinical zones. Any combined Türwächter + powered locking configuration must comply with EN 13637 fail-safe release. IoT module alone is compliant (supervisory only); combined system requires integrator-level certification.

| Mitigation action | Detail |
|---|---|
| Define Türwächter integration wiring standard | Publish wiring spec that isolates Türwächter alarm trigger from all powered lock control paths |
| System integrator EN 13637 sign-off | Require certified integrator to produce combined-system compliance documentation for restricted zones |
| Zone classification | Map hospital doors by zone type: Türwächter-only (standard) vs. combined access control (requires EN 13637 sign-off) |

**Cost:** medium ($10K–$100K depending on number of combined configurations) | **Time:** 3–6 months

---

sections.title: Mitigation Cost Summary
sections.content:
| Total mitigation burden | Assessment |
|---|---|
| Knockout constraints | 0 |
| High-cost mitigations (>$100K) | 0 |
| Medium-cost mitigations ($10K–$100K) | 4 (C3, C6, C7, C10) |
| Low-cost mitigations (<$10K) | 3 (C4, C8, C9) |
| None | 3 (C1, C2, C5) |

Hospital entry requires primarily **integration investment** (C6 BACnet bridge, C10 EN 13637 integrator engagement) and **compliance documentation** (C8 GDPR/HIPAA), not hardware redesign. The product's mechanical-primary architecture pre-satisfies the one absolute constraint (C2), making hospital markets accessible with medium engineering and compliance effort.

---
